title: LMDB has no engine-side page-cipher seam — boot invariant is the right floor
content:
When this codebase set out to wire `EncryptedPageStream` into the LMDB catalog (heed-backed), the seam survey turned up nothing usable:

- `mdb_env_set_userctx` only stores a userdata pointer; LMDB's actual page IO does not consult it.
- A `heed::Env` wrapper that intercepts `RoTxn::get`/`RwTxn::put` could re-encode user-visible bytes, but LMDB keeps stale MVCC page snapshots inside the database file. A wrapper that only touches user-visible bytes leaves plaintext residue in the MVCC pages.
- A real fix is one of: fork heed/LMDB to add a page-cipher hook (multi-week, upstream-fragile); replace the catalog backend with a non-LMDB store (multi-month); accept filesystem-level encryption (LUKS/dm-crypt/BitLocker) as the standard solution for LMDB-class engines.

Similar story for the mmap-backed record stores (`adjacency_list.rs`, `property_store.rs`): they mutate `MmapMut` bytes in place. AES-GCM is not a stream cipher and the per-page nonce changes on every overwrite, so any in-place mutation breaks encryption. The standard fix is a buffer pool — pages live in heap-allocated buffers, encrypted on write-back — but that is the storage-hot-path refactor that takes years.

The page cache (`crates/nexus-core/src/page_cache/mod.rs`) does not back to disk yet (`flush`/`evict_page` carry "In real implementation: write to disk" comments at lines 313, 383, 398). Wiring EaR into a cache that has no IO seam is meaningless work.

The right floor that ships value today is a **boot-time inventory scanner** that walks the data directory, classifies every file by the EaR magic, and rejects mixed-mode databases. The scanner runs before the executor opens any record store; it ships at `crates/nexus-core/src/storage/crypto/inventory.rs`. The moment the storage-layer refactor lands, the same scanner inherits the new files and detects the cutover automatically.
